perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall, and shall cause its Representatives to, keep the Confidential Information in confidence and shall not disclose any of the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; provided , however , that (i) the Receiving Party may make disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Information if required by applicable law, regulation or legal or regulatory process; provided, that the Receiving Party shall have first complied with the terms of Section 9 hereof, (ii) the Receiving Party may make disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Information to the extent that the Company gives its prior written consent, and (iii) any information contained in the Confidential Information may be disclosed to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who reasonably require access to such information for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction and who agree to keep such information in confidence to the same extent as described herein; provided , further , that the Receiving Party shall not make any disclosure of any Confidential Information to any potential sources of equity financing without the Company’s prior written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall endeavor to cause its Representatives to keep the Confidential Information in confidence and shall not disclose any of the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; provided , however , that (i) the Receiving Party may make disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Information if generally required by applicable law, regulation or legal or regulatory process; provided, that the Receiving Party attempts to comply with the terms of Section 9 hereof, (ii) the Receiving Party may make disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Information to the extent that the Company seems to give its prior written consent, and (iii) any information contained in the Confidential Information may be disclosed to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who appear to reasonably require access to such information for the purpose of potentially evaluating a possible Transaction and who indicate agreement to keep such information in confidence to some extent as described herein; provided , further , that the Receiving Party shall avoid making any disclosure of any Confidential Information to any potential sources of equity financing without a verbal indication of the Company’s consent.
perturbation.explanation: The changes weaken the obligation to maintain confidentiality. Replacing 'shall' with 'shall endeavor' and introducing qualifiers like 'generally,' 'attempts to comply,' 'seems to give,' 'appear to reasonably require,' 'potentially evaluating,' 'indicate agreement to some extent,' and 'verbal indication' create ambiguity and make enforcement difficult. The original text ensures strict compliance, while the modified text introduces subjective elements and uncertainties.
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation FD requires selective disclosures to be public and broadly disseminated, and ensures that all investors have equal access to material information. The modified text's ambiguity about disclosing confidential information, especially the 'verbal indication' of consent for equity financing disclosures, contradicts Regulation FD because it allows for potentially selective and non-public disclosures based on informal approvals, rather than clear written consent and public dissemination. Disclosing information with only a 'verbal indication' does not ensure the company adheres to Regulation FD.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract language introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality, potentially contradicting Regulation FD. The original text mandated strict confidentiality, while the revised version uses terms like 'endeavor,' 'generally,' 'attempts to comply,' 'seems to give,' 'appear to reasonably require,' 'potentially evaluating,' 'indicate agreement to some extent,' and 'verbal indication.' These changes undermine the legally enforceable commitment to confidentiality. The Law Explanation notes that the 'verbal indication' of consent for equity financing disclosures contradicts Regulation FD by allowing for selective and non-public disclosures. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions '17 CFR 243.100 -- General rule regarding selective disclosure,' indicating a focus on preventing selective disclosure, which the modified contract weakens, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that, for a period of eighteen months from the date of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall not (and shall cause its affiliates, subject to Section 22 hereof, not to), directly or indirectly, (i) use the Confidential Information to divert or attempt to divert any business or customer of the Company or (ii) employ or solicit, or initiate contact for employment with, any (A) director, officer or other senior or key employee of the Company or (B) any other employee of the Company whom the Receiving Party meets during its evaluation of the possible Transaction or about whom the Receiving Party receives Confidential Information; provided , however , a general advertisement or other recruiting efforts not 2 specifically targeting any such employees of the Company shall not be considered a solicitation or unauthorized hiring.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party generally agrees that, for a period of roughly eighteen months from the date of this Agreement, the Receiving Party will attempt to cause its affiliates, subject to Section 22 hereof, not to), directly or indirectly, (i) use the Confidential Information to possibly divert any business or customer of the Company or (ii) often employ or solicit, or vaguely initiate contact for employment with, any (A) director, officer or other relatively senior or somewhat key employee of the Company or (B) any other employee of the Company whom the Receiving Party may meet during its evaluation of the possible Transaction or about whom the Receiving Party receives Confidential Information; provided , however , a general advertisement or other recruiting efforts occasionally not specifically targeting any such employees of the Company will not definitively be considered a solicitation or unauthorized hiring.
perturbation.explanation: Weakening the language with words like 'generally,' 'roughly,' 'attempt,' 'possibly,' 'often,' 'vaguely,' 'relatively,' 'somewhat,' 'may,' 'occasionally,' and 'definitively' introduces ambiguity and makes the non-compete and non-solicitation clauses much less enforceable. The original text provides a clear prohibition; the modified version creates uncertainty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding non-compete agreements (Delaware Code Title 19, Section 708)
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law requires that non-compete agreements be reasonable in scope and duration to be enforceable. The modifications introduce vagueness regarding the agreement's enforceability, particularly concerning the restricted activities and the definition of employees. The use of words like "possibly divert," "often employ," and "somewhat key employee" creates uncertainty about the scope of the restrictions, which could render the agreement unreasonable and unenforceable under Delaware law. Delaware courts scrutinize non-compete agreements, and ambiguity regarding scope could lead a court to deem it unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The original contract contains clear prohibitions, while the changed contract introduces ambiguity that weakens the enforceability of the non-compete and non-solicitation clauses, using terms like 'generally,' 'roughly,' 'attempt,' 'possibly,' 'often,' 'vaguely,' 'relatively,' 'somewhat,' 'may,' 'occasionally,' and 'definitively.' The LLM-generated Law Explanation states that Delaware law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in scope and duration to be enforceable, and that ambiguity regarding scope could lead a court to deem it unenforceable. Snippet 1 does not directly address non-compete agreements or their enforceability, but it does underscore that Delaware law includes specific regulations regarding employment practices. The weakening of the obligation, introduces ambiguity making it more difficult to enforce, and thus creates a legal contradiction under the definition provided.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party will return all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents or materials prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect any Confidential Information shall be destroyed. Notwithstanding the return to the Company or destruction of Confidential Information pursuant to this Section 7, the Receiving Party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by their confidentiality obligations and other obligations under this Agreement for the term 4 hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon a request of the Company, the Receiving Party will try to return some copies of what it considers to be the Confidential Information to the Company, and some notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents or materials prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that vaguely contain or reflect any Confidential Information should perhaps be destroyed. Notwithstanding the return to the Company or destruction of Confidential Information pursuant to this Section 7, the Receiving Party and its Representatives will seemingly continue to be bound by their confidentiality obligations and other obligations under this Agreement for a period of time.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Promptly upon the written request' to 'Upon a request', 'will return all copies' to 'will try to return some copies of what it considers to be', 'contain or reflect' to 'vaguely contain or reflect', 'shall be destroyed' to 'should perhaps be destroyed', and 'continue to be bound' to 'seemingly continue to be bound', weakens the obligation to return and destroy confidential information. The duration of the ongoing confidentiality obligations is also made unclear.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York State Trade Secrets Law
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law protects trade secrets. If the 'Confidential Information' qualifies as trade secrets, failing to ensure the complete return or destruction of such information, as now made ambiguous, undermines the protections afforded under New York trade secret law. The Receiving Party's obligation to maintain confidentiality is crucial for trade secret protection, and by weakening this obligation, the modified text contradicts the intent and purpose of New York trade secret law.
perturbation.justification: The original contract ensures a prompt and complete return or destruction of confidential information, maintaining a clear obligation. The changed text introduces ambiguity by using terms like 'try to return some copies of what it considers to be,' 'vaguely contain or reflect,' and 'should perhaps be destroyed,' which weaken the obligation to protect confidential information. The Law Explanation argues that this undermines New York trade secret law. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not directly related to trade secrets, the Perturbation Explanation states that the modifications weaken the legal obligation. This weakening of the obligation, turning it into a subjective 'try' and 'perhaps,' creates an ambiguity that makes the contract difficult to enforce, thus establishing a legal contradiction under the definition provided.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Receiving Party or anyone to whom it discloses the Confidential Information receives a request to disclose all or any part of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information under the terms of a subpoena or other order issued by a court of competent jurisdiction or by another governmental agency, the Receiving Party shall if permitted pursuant to applicable law (a) promptly notify the Company of the existence, terms and circumstances surrounding such a request, (b) consult with the Company on the advisability of taking steps to resist or narrow such request, (c) if disclosure of such Confidential Information or Discussion Information is required, furnish only such portion of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information as the Receiving Party is advised by its legal counsel is legally required to be disclosed and (d) if requested by the Company, cooperate with the Company in its efforts to obtain a protective order or other relief to prevent the disclosure of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information or other reliable assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded to such portion of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information, as applicable, that is required to be disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Receiving Party or anyone to whom it discloses the Confidential Information receives something resembling a request to disclose some part of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information under potentially the terms of a subpoena or order from what appears to be a court or governmental agency, the Receiving Party will attempt if reasonably permitted by law to (a) notify the Company somewhat promptly of the general existence of such a request, (b) consider consulting with the Company on whether to resist such request, (c) if disclosure seems required, furnish only what the Receiving Party feels it must disclose based on what its legal counsel loosely advises and (d) if vaguely requested by the Company, maybe cooperate with the Company's possible efforts to obtain some relief to prevent disclosing the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'a request' to 'something resembling a request', 'the terms' to 'potentially the terms', 'a court of competent jurisdiction or by another governmental agency' to 'what appears to be a court or governmental agency', 'shall if permitted pursuant to applicable law' to 'will attempt if reasonably permitted by law', 'promptly notify' to 'notify somewhat promptly', 'the existence, terms and circumstances' to 'the general existence', 'consult with' to 'consider consulting with', 'is required' to 'seems required', 'as the Receiving Party is advised by its legal counsel is legally required to be disclosed' to 'what the Receiving Party feels it must disclose based on what its legal counsel loosely advises', 'if requested by the Company, cooperate' to 'if vaguely requested by the Company, maybe cooperate', 'efforts to obtain a protective order or other relief' to 'possible efforts to obtain some relief', the obligation to protect confidential information when facing a subpoena or court order is significantly weakened and made ambiguous.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 45(d)(3) outlines the protections afforded to those subject to subpoenas, including the requirement to protect trade secrets and confidential information. The modified language makes the receiving party's obligation to notify the company, consult on resistance, limit disclosure, and cooperate in obtaining protective orders highly discretionary and vague. This weakens the protections and could lead to the improper disclosure of confidential information that should be protected under FRCP 45(d)(3). The ambiguity undermines the party's responsibility to reasonably protect trade secrets and confidential information from being disclosed during discovery.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changes introduce ambiguity weakening the legal obligation to protect confidential information when facing a subpoena or court order. The original text outlines specific actions the Receiving Party 'shall' take, while the modified text uses vague language, such as 'something resembling a request', 'potentially the terms', 'what appears to be a court or governmental agency', 'will attempt if reasonably permitted by law', 'notify somewhat promptly', 'general existence', 'consider consulting', 'seems required', 'what the Receiving Party feels it must disclose based on what its legal counsel loosely advises', 'if vaguely requested by the Company, maybe cooperate', and 'possible efforts to obtain some relief'. This makes the obligations discretionary and undermines the responsibility to protect confidential information, as explained in the Law Explanation. The ambiguity introduced contradicts the intent of FRCP 45(d)(3) to protect trade secrets and confidential information during discovery, as mentioned in the Law Explanation.
